m HE telephone bell rang and a
Il line of eager faces crowded


II about the rail. They were
dirty faces, some of them, and some
were unshaven, but as whole they
were the faces of honest men. The
InUn at the telephone said some
thing about "chopping wood" and
"35 ce7ks an hour" He turned to-
ward the line of men-some were
mere boys-and a score of hands
were held up like SO many scholars
in a school room anxious to answer
question propounded by the
teacher.


You'll do" said the man behind
the counter, pointing to tall for
eigner, who grinned his apprecia-
ton. The others turned away to
await another telephone call. The
lucky one gave his name, received
~ Iof paper and was off in a
hurry


The scene was at the Spokane
free employment bureau and it
might have been any hour of the
day, for the same thing is enacted
repeatedly from opening till clos
ing time. The Jingle of the tele-
phone bell is the signal to the score
scriptiOns Who constantly wait
or more~of men of all ages and de-
about the door and in the corridor
of the city hall basement, ready to
line up before the railing for the
job which they know is to be had
from the man at the other end of
the wire.


SUPPLIES ASSORTED JOBS.


M. r. Arant, manager of the bu-
reau, supplies jobs-mostly odd
jobs of day or few hours-to
about so or 80 men a day. The
city free employment agency-it
charges the employer and employee
nothing for it's services-supplies
work monthly to hundreds of work.
ing men who are for the time un-
employed, and is the means of set
ting many a "down-and-outer" on
his feet again. 1t supplies a class
of assorted jobs which privately
operated agencies can not, for the
reason that it would not pay them
to handle them.


The Spokane citizen with lawn
to rake, wood to chop or coal to
put in, as well as the business
